_metadata.source: Appel, McCrory, and Tamkin. 'Anthropic Economic Index Report: Uneven Geographic and Enterprise AI Adoption.' Anthropic, September 15, 2025.
_metadata.dataCollection: Privacy-preserving analysis of 1 million Claude.ai Free and Pro conversations from August 4–11, 2025, randomly sampled. API data from 1P API customers analyzed separately.
_metadata.measure: Anthropic AI Usage Index (AUI) = share of Claude usage / share of working-age population (ages 15–64). AUI > 1 indicates higher usage than expected; AUI < 1 indicates lower usage.
notes: Excludes conversations flagged as potential trust and safety violations.
Excludes conversations originating from VPN, anycast, or hosting services.
Unit of observation is a conversation, not a user.

usStateAUI.totalUSUsageShareNote: US dominates total usage at 21.6% of all Claude.ai conversations.

global.description: A 1% increase in GDP per capita is associated with a 0.7% increase in Claude usage per capita across countries.

usStates.description: A 1% increase in state GDP per capita is associated with a 1.8% increase in the AI Usage Index across US states.
usStates.varianceNote: Income differences explain less than half the variation in cross-state adoption rates. At the global level, the source identifies several potential contributing factors beyond income: digital infrastructure, economic structure, regulatory environment, awareness and access, and trust and comfort (pp. 17–18).

useCaseDiversification.pattern: Higher per capita Claude usage is associated with more diverse task usage. Lower adoption countries concentrate overwhelmingly on coding tasks.

diversifyingCategories._note: The geographic pattern (Figure 2.7, pp. 21–22) shows these categories growing as AUI increases across countries. The specific percentages below are Chapter 1 temporal trends (p. 3, V1→V3 sample change) that contextualize the geographic pattern.
withTemporalData: Education (rising from 9.3% to 12.4% globally — Chapter 1 temporal trend, p. 3)
Science (rising from 6.3% to 7.2% globally — Chapter 1 temporal trend, p. 3)

collaborationPatterns.pattern: As Claude usage per capita increases, countries shift from automation-focused to augmentation-focused usage, even when controlling for task mix.
collaborationPatterns.interpretation: Mature markets tend to use AI more collaboratively, while emerging markets are more likely to delegate complete tasks to it — possibly reflecting differences in how AI is deployed by economies at different stages of structural transformation.
collaborationPatterns.methodNote: Relationship holds after controlling for differences in task composition across countries (partial regression analysis on residuals).

regressionStats.note: Partial regression controlling for task mix. β represents the change in automation share residual per unit increase in AUI residual.

censusData._source: Figure 3.1, p. 32; Census Bureau Business Trends and Outlook Survey

adoptionRate.description: AI adoption among US firms more than doubled in two years, from 3.7% to 9.7%. Despite rapid growth, the vast majority of firms do not report using AI.

censusData.sectorVariation: One in four businesses in the Information sector reported using AI — roughly ten times the rate for Accommodation and Food Services.

patterns: Businesses use Claude in more specialized ways than individual users.
Enterprise usage concentrates in tasks suited for programmatic access (coding, administrative tasks).
Businesses use Claude less for educational or creative tasks.
Enterprise usage is automation-dominant at both the transcript and task level (see automationDetail).
Nearly half of all API traffic maps to computer and mathematical tasks — 8+ percentage points higher than Claude.ai consumer usage.

transcriptLevel._note: Percentage of API transcripts showing automation vs. augmentation patterns. Remaining ~11% unclassified.

taskLevel._note: Percentage of O*NET tasks that show automation-dominant patterns. A stronger claim than the transcript-level figure: nearly all tasks performed through the API are automation-dominant, compared to fewer than half on Claude.ai (Figure 3.5).

topAPIUseCases._note: Cluster names and percentages are exact values from Figure 3.2 (bottom-up taxonomy of 1P API transcripts by usage share).

categories.category: Resolve software development technical issues and workflow problems
Debug and develop web application frontend code and components
Develop and manage professional business software and service systems
Develop, configure and evaluate AI systems across domains
Software development troubleshooting and optimization assistance
Create professional marketing, business, and journalistic content
Develop and troubleshoot data extraction and analytics systems
Develop data extraction and conversion tools for multiple sources
Develop and debug automated software testing infrastructure
Web security assessment and digital communications protection
Develop financial software and trading analysis tools
Develop and troubleshoot software authentication and access control systems
Process and evaluate professional business and recruitment data
Design and develop web interface UI/UX elements
Implement and troubleshoot software database and search functionality

categoryShifts._note: Direct comparisons showing how task category shares differ between Claude.ai consumer usage and 1P API enterprise usage.

contextBottleneck.finding: API customers that use Claude for complex tasks tend to provide Claude with lengthy inputs. Access to appropriate contextual information is needed for sophisticated deployment.
contextBottleneck.implication: This could represent a barrier to broader enterprise deployment for some important tasks that rely on dispersed context not already centralized or digitized. Correcting for this bottleneck may require firms to restructure their organization, invest in new data infrastructure, and centralize information for effective model deployment.
contextBottleneck.institutionalInertia: Institutional inertia, alongside fixed costs of adoption, suggests that early enterprise AI use is likely to be concentrated among specialized tasks where deployment is easy, capabilities are robust, and the economic benefits from adoption are high.

keyComparisonPairs._note: Pre-computed comparison sets for use in Module 1 interactive data narratives and Module 2 dashboard.
keyComparisonPairs.portfolioNarrative: For an organization operating teams across Singapore (4.57), Jakarta (0.36), and Mumbai (0.27), this is not merely a market-level observation — it is an internal capability gap.
